_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Rockbridge County, Virginia. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the VMI-and-iron county where Hunter's 1864 raid burned Lexington but spared the courthouse records.

current_courthouse.location: Lexington, VA — 2 South Main Street (replaced the antebellum courthouse that survived the Civil War)

previous_courthouses.location: Lexington — original log courthouse near the present courthouse square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a more substantial structure as the county's population grew.

previous_courthouses.location: Lexington — brick courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Survived Hunter's 1864 burning of Lexington. While VMI and Governor Letcher's home burned, the courthouse was not targeted. Records survived intact. Replaced in 1897 by the current building.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Hunter's burning of Lexington
record_losses.description: Hunter's Union force occupied Lexington in June 1864 and burned VMI, Governor Letcher's home, and other properties. Remarkably, the courthouse was spared — the intervention of Washington College president and other citizens may have prevented its destruction. The bound record books survived intact. The courthouse records are continuous from the county's formation in 1778, making Rockbridge one of the best-documented counties in the Valley.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Rockbridge County's records survived a burning that destroyed VMI and much of Lexington. If {ancestor_name}'s records are missing from Rockbridge, the gap predates the war — pre-1778 records are in Augusta County, and iron furnace records (account books, time books) are scattered across private collections and university archives.
record_losses.source: Heatwole, John L. The Burning: Sheridan in the Shenandoah Valley. Charlottesville: Rockbridge Publishing, 1998.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Pre-1778 deeds are in Augusta County at Staunton.
deeds.repository: Rockbridge County Circuit Court, Lexington; Library of Virginia microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Virginia land records; Library of Virginia digital collections

wills_estates.gaps: Bound will books are intact. Some loose estate papers from the Revolutionary era are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: Rockbridge County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation; minister returns are scattered before 1853
marriages.gaps: Some Scots-Irish Presbyterian marriages performed by itinerant ministers may not appear in civil records. Marriage registers begin 1853.
marriages.repository: Rockbridge County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — County Court order books are nearly continuous
court_minutes.gaps: The order books contain detailed records of the iron industry — furnace leases, ore rights, and labor disputes — that supplement the deed books for understanding the county's industrial economy.
court_minutes.repository: Rockbridge County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia microfilm
court_minutes.online_access: Library of Virginia microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Fair — compliance was uneven, particularly in the mountain districts. The 1853–1896 registers survive but are incomplete. Gap from 1896–1912 is nearly total. Lexington city may have better coverage than rural Rockbridge.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records; Library of Virginia for pre-1896 registers

substitute_sources.description: Rockbridge County was formed from Augusta County in 1778. All records before that date are at Staunton, documented in Chalkley's Chronicles.
substitute_sources.repository: Augusta County Circuit Court, Staunton; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any Rockbridge family tracing back to the frontier period. Chalkley's three-volume abstract covers this material comprehensively.
substitute_sources.source: Chalkley, Lyman. Chronicles of the Scotch-Irish Settlement in Virginia. 3 vols. Rosslyn, VA: Commonwealth Printing, 1912.

substitute_sources.source_type: Iron furnace account books and time books
substitute_sources.description: The iron furnaces of Rockbridge County — Lucy Selina, Bath, Gibraltar, Buffalo Forge, and others — kept detailed account books recording workers (both free and enslaved), production, and supplies. Some survive in private collections and university archives.
substitute_sources.repository: Special Collections, Washington and Lee University; Virginia Museum of History and Culture; Duke University Special Collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Iron furnace records are critical for documenting enslaved people in Rockbridge County. Charles Dew's study of Buffalo Forge demonstrates that furnace records can be more detailed than plantation records — enslaved ironworkers were sometimes named, their skills recorded, and their output tracked individually.
substitute_sources.source: Dew, Charles B. Bond of Iron: Master and Slave at Buffalo Forge. New York: W.W. Norton, 1994.

substitute_sources.source_type: Washington and Lee University and VMI archives
substitute_sources.description: Both institutions hold manuscript collections relevant to Rockbridge County genealogy, including personal papers of faculty and local families, student records, and Civil War correspondence.
substitute_sources.repository: Special Collections, James G. Leyburn Library, Washington and Lee University; VMI Archives, Lexington
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Faculty and trustee papers often contain family correspondence and business records. VMI cadet records document young men from across the Valley. Robert E. Lee's presidency of Washington College (1865–1870) generated extensive correspondence now in the university archives.
substitute_sources.source: Washington and Lee University Special Collections finding aids.

substitute_sources.source_type: Presbyterian church records — Timber Ridge, New Providence, Lexington
substitute_sources.description: Rockbridge County's Scots-Irish population was overwhelmingly Presbyterian. The Timber Ridge and New Providence churches, among the oldest in the Valley, kept session minutes, baptism records, and membership rolls from the 1740s.
substitute_sources.repository: Union Theological Seminary Archives, Richmond; Historical Foundation, Montreat, NC; individual churches
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Presbyterian records are essential for Rockbridge families. Timber Ridge Church records predate the county itself and document families from the earliest settlement. Session minutes record births, deaths, marriages, and church discipline.
substitute_sources.source: Foote, William Henry. Sketches of Virginia, Historical and Biographical. Philadelphia: William S. Martien, 1850.

narrative_hooks: When Hunter's troops burned VMI in June 1864, the Rockbridge County courthouse was spared — and {ancestor_name}'s deeds, wills, and court records survived a day that destroyed much of Lexington.

If {ancestor_name} was connected to one of Rockbridge County's iron furnaces, the furnace account books — sometimes held at Washington and Lee University or private collections — may document enslaved workers by name and skill when plantation records recorded only numbers.

Before 1778, {ancestor_name}'s Rockbridge County records are at Staunton in Augusta County — and Chalkley's Chronicles may already have abstracted the relevant court orders, estate inventories, and land transactions.

The Timber Ridge Presbyterian Church records predate Rockbridge County itself — if {ancestor_name} was Scots-Irish Presbyterian, the church session minutes may record family events the courthouse never documented.

Virginia's vital registration gap (1896–1912) means that births and deaths in rural Rockbridge during those years left no civil record — the family Bible, the church register, and the cemetery stone are the only evidence.

Rockbridge County's court order books document a dual economy — wheat farms on the valley floor and iron furnaces on the mountain edge — and {ancestor_name}'s court appearances may reveal which world the family occupied.
source_references: Heatwole, John L. The Burning: Sheridan in the Shenandoah Valley. Charlottesville: Rockbridge Publishing, 1998.
Dew, Charles B. Bond of Iron: Master and Slave at Buffalo Forge. New York: W.W. Norton, 1994.
Chalkley, Lyman. Chronicles of the Scotch-Irish Settlement in Virginia. 3 vols. Rosslyn, VA: Commonwealth Printing, 1912.
Foote, William Henry. Sketches of Virginia, Historical and Biographical. Philadelphia: William S. Martien, 1850.
Robertson, James I., Jr. Stonewall Jackson: The Man, the Soldier, the Legend. New York: Macmillan, 1997.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, various editions.
